name: Israfil
description: Israfil, also known as Sarafiel, is an archangel revered in Islam. He is primarily known for his role as the angel who will signal the Day of Judgment by blowing the trumpet, an event described in the Quran and elaborated upon in Islamic traditions. Depictions of Israfil often portray him as a towering figure with immense wings and a trumpet perpetually held to his lips, awaiting God's command. While not explicitly mentioned in the Quran by name, interpretations link him to references of angels tasked with specific duties related to the end times. His significance lies in his eschatological role, embodying the divine power and the inevitability of resurrection and judgment. Sunni traditions often place him among the four major archangels, alongside Jibril (Gabriel), Mika'il (Michael), and Azrael. Accounts of his creation vary, but he is generally understood to be among the first angels created by God.
subtitle: The Trumpeter of Doom

keyMyths.title: The Blowing of the Trumpet
keyMyths.description: Israfil's most significant role is blowing the trumpet to signal the Day of Judgment. The first blow will bring about the destruction of the world and the death of all living beings. A second blow will then resurrect all souls for judgment before God. This event is a cornerstone of Islamic eschatology and serves as a reminder of accountability for one's actions in life. The precise timing and nature of these blows are subjects of theological discussion and interpretation.

keyMyths.title: Israfil's Constant Vigil
keyMyths.description: It is believed that Israfil stands eternally before God, his trumpet poised and ready, awaiting the divine command to initiate the Day of Judgment. This constant state of readiness highlights the unwavering obedience of angels to God's will and the imminence of the end times. Some accounts describe Israfil's eyes constantly fixed upon the Divine Throne, awaiting the signal.

keyMyths.title: Israfil's Role in Creation
keyMyths.description: Some traditions suggest Israfil played a role in the creation of the universe and the recording of events. He is sometimes associated with the celestial music and the harmony of the spheres, reflecting the divine order and beauty of creation. This role connects him to the broader cosmic narrative beyond his eschatological function. His role in creation, however, is less emphasized compared to his role in the end times.

sources.author: Wheeler, Brannon M.
sources.work: Prophets in the Quran: An Introduction to the Quran and Muslim Exegesis
sources.description: Provides context for understanding the roles of angels in Islamic theology and Quranic interpretation.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding the roles of angels in Islamic theology and Quranic interpretation.
